CURRENT EAFFAIRS,

George ‘‘ the Saved ’’ has been interviewed by a re-
presentative of the British Daily Chronicle. The
amusing British ignorance of Irish affairs was displayed
by the Daily Clronicle’s young man in the opening
sentence of his ‘‘copy,”’ for therein George ‘‘the
Saved ’’ is designated ‘‘ The Novelist and Gaclic lte-
wvivalist.””  The italics are our own joke. The inter-
viewer found George ‘‘ the Saved ’’ reading ‘‘ Pilgrim’s
Progress.”” That little ““scene’’ we should say was
arranged. It is encouraging to read that George de-
clared ‘T never had any education myself, and I am
now not an educated man.” It is encouraging, for
one often fears that education is overrated and is looked
upon too much as a panacea, and the fact that Georgo
was never educated acts as a corrective by offering con-
clusive evidonce that education has a vast deal to be
said for it. This  Gaelic Revivalist *’ on being askod

—‘“ Have you learned Irish yourselff replied ‘“ No,

T haven’t, I have many other things to do.” George

declared that ‘“the wretched National Board’”’ was
composed of ‘‘second-rate people.”’  George is cer-
tainly first class—of his own sort. Later on in the
interview, as reported in the British ha’penny paper,
George “the Saved’ crupted in great “saved”
form. “Don’t you think the educational outlook in
this country is improving?’ asked the British paper’s
young man. ‘‘No, I don’t,”’ was the answer. ‘‘There
are so many things against it. The Catholicity of the
country, for example. Catholicity is sterile. It pro-
duces no literature. Why? Because all the avenues
are blocked. At most of them the priests have put up
signboards, ‘ This way leads to Hell.” The demand for
Catholic Universities means that the priests want to
control the human mind.”” No doubt the ha’penny
British public enjoyed that, and regard our only
George, who says truly that he was never educated, as

a most ‘‘ enlightened *’ man.

Recently the London(derry) Board of Guardians had
the appointing of a nurse before them. There were
three applicants, one ‘“saved ’’ and two ‘ Idolators’’;
almost needless to say the ‘“saved’’ was appointed.
Subsequently it was decided to advertise for four pro-
bationer nurses. ‘“ You can add to the advertisement
that no Catholic need apply,”” was the caustic comment
of one of the guardians, Mrs. Morris. At this meeting
a Mr. R. N. Anderson proposed the appointment of
the “saved’’ nurse and apparently sought to support
his proposition on the ground of fairness! Mrs.
Morris, in a letter to a local paper, throws some light
on the kind of fairness doled out to ‘‘ Idolators’’ by
the London(derry) Board of Guardians.

Mrs. Morris, before going into tabulated particulars,
says: —‘‘ Owing to the dogged interruption I met with
in my endeavour to reply to Mr. Anderson’s appeal
for fair play for Protestant officials in the Derry Union
on Saturday last I was deprived of the privilege of
showing the relative position of Protestant and Catholic
officials therein. The following will show the necessity
for the appeal:’” Well, ‘“the following’’ comprises
a detailed list of the names, occupations and salaries of
the salaried officials. We need not give our readers
in all parts of Ireland the detailed list, suffice it to
say that ‘“saved’ officials draw £2,475 6s. 0d. per
annum and base ‘‘Idolator’’ officials the paltry sum
of £295 0s. 0d. Murs. Margaret Morris concludes her
letter in this way:—‘ Out of the entire sum of
£2,770 6s. 0d. paid officials there reaches Catholics the
magnificent amount of £295, about £100 for every
thousand paid the Protestant officials! And Mr.
Anderson wants fair play for the Protestants! When
I becamo a Guardian six years ago there were only twe
Catholic officials in the union—Mrs. uiuespie (nurse)
and Denis Quirk (boilerman). The other six officials,
i.e., the schoolmaster and five district dispensary
nurses, are recent appointments. The foregoing state-
ment will show who had the greatest reason to ask for
fair play on Saturday last.”’

What are ‘‘ Idolators’’ of Derry doing in the cause
of Tolerance, Justico and Fair Play. Surely there is
plonty work at their hands if they had the grit to
tackle it. Why don’t they organise up there?

We have received the fourth annual report and 
statement of accounts of the Irish Drapers' Assistants 
Benefit and Protective Association. 
The receipts for 
the year amounted to £2,800 7s. Id., whilst the ex-
penditure, including £1,219 3s. 9d. paid in benefits, 
totalled £2,311 8s. 7d., leaving a balance of £4.88 18s. 
Gd., which, with the balance of £1,712 8s. 9cl. in hand 
on 31st August, 1904, makes a total balance to· credit 
of £2,201 7s. 3d. 
Six new branches were started, 865 
new members enrolled, and the total eITective member-
ship on the 31st August of this year was 2,750. 
Dur-
ing the year there was a leakage in membership of 
385 as against the 865 new members. 
Of the 385 
seventeen died, 24 started business for themselves, 61 
left the trade, 39 emigrated, 45 lapsed, and 198 arc 
unaccounted for. 
In our last issue we gave an interesting short story 
concerning the Great "Sick" and Sourface Railway, 
and promised another in the current issue. 
The second 
short story is more interesting, as there is a lady as 
well as a bicycle in the case :-On the 2nd of June a 
lady's bicycle was damaged at the Cork station of the 
Great "Sick" and Sourface Railway, and she claimed 
22s. 6d. damage. 
On the 19th of that month Mr. Bul-

thoroughly investigated the matter, and learn that the 
Company is not, in any way, to blame. 
As the bicycle 
was not given in charge to any of the Company's offi-
cials, it was left on the platform on your own risk. 
I 
am therefore instructed to inform you that your claim 
cannot be entertained."

Mr. J. H Bell, imported Superintendent of the
Great “Sick *’ and Sourface Railway, rotorted on tho
28th September that he had nothing further to add to
his communication of the 21st, and on Saturday,
September 30th, the passenger wrote:-—“ By your lot-
ter of the 28th inst., I note you have nothing further
to add re my sister’s claim. Might I suggest that you
made a mistake in paying my claim of 6s. (Ref. 1896),
as according to your decision in my sister’s claim you
were not liable for the delay to my machine. The mat-
ter seems to me as follows:—You delayed my machine
in transit from Dublin to Killeagh (Co. Cork); I had
to go to some expense, and a lot of trouble on that
account, and you paid my claim of 6s. Very fair.
You smashed my sister’s bicycle at Cork station (for
which a porter was dismissed); she had to go to ex-
pense on that account. You agree to pay for the
damage, but you are not liable for the expense. Very
unfair.  Under the circumstances, might I inquire
what is the difference in the claims? and if you are
still bent on sticking to the offer of 10s. %’

The Department (Scotch) is playing the martinet,
the obstructionist and the bigot in its relation to the
Technical Committee of Tralee, and things, as a conse-
quence, are at a deadlock. It is almost needless to say
that there is a Scotchman in the case. We have waded
through some long newspaper reports of meetings of the
Tralee Technical Committee, but as the Dublin Castle
New Department plays the obscurant part, it is diffi-
cult to draw any definite conclusion from the report of
the proceedings beyond the very definite one that the
Department (Scotch) plays with Scotchmen and West
Britons up its sleeve. The matters at issue in Tralee
have more than a local interest. At the request of the
Tralee Committee,, and with the approval of the
almighty Castle Board, the New Department, M.
Quinlan qualified himself as teacher of a Mathematics
class. He has taught the class successfully for three
years. Neither tho Technical Committee nor the high-
salaried, first-class railway travelling martinets of the
New Dublin Castle Department, of which Mr. Walter
Long, the Orangemen’s Chief Secrctary, is President,
found any fault with him. An imported Scotchman,
a Mr. Horne, is head master of the Technical Schools.
Mr. Quinlan, an Irishman, as his name implies, has
also the economic disadvantage of being an “ Idolator ”’
from out of the ranks of which much criticised and
be-lectured body successful Intermediate students, if
not “experts,”” come from. Horne’s qualification in
Mathematics, we gather, is second-class certificate in
Elementary stage. Quinlan’s is first-class in- Second
stage ; Quinlan, the “ Idolator,”” is now—if the Depart-
ment over which Walter Long, the Orangemen’s Chief
Secretary, presides has its way—to be dismissed, and
Horne, the “saved’’ Scotchman, is to take up his
class!

The Tralee Technical Committee recently gave Mr.
Horne and others a rise of salary, and the Department
(Scotch) sanctioned fthe rise to the Scotchman, but
decreed that he should take over Quinlan’s class, and
that Mr. Quinlan, the Irish “Idolator,”” should be dis-
missed. The proposed increase to the Scotch importa-
tion was last on the Committee’s agenda, but by somo
accident or other, which the Scotch mathematician
might be able to'explain, Mr. Horne’s proposed “ riso "’
was first on the list of proposed increases as presented
to the Department (Scotch).

The Technical Committee of Tralee refused to fall in
with the views of the New Department (Scotch) over

. which Walter Long presides, and so one Mr. Turnbull,

an official, was sent down—we presume in a first-class
carriage—to Tralee. Father Buckley, at a meeting at

THE LEADER. 115

which Mr. Turnbull, of the New Castle Department,
was present, proceeded to refer to the qualifications of
Mr. Horno, the Scotch importation. “You need not
go into tho qualifications; the Department have dealt
with that,”” said Mr. Turnbull on behalf of the Depart-
ment that wants to turn Paddy from the job in favour
of Sandy. Later on Iather Buckley asked— At whose
instance was Mr. Horne’s name put down as teacher of.
Mathematics?” And the lofty Mr. Turnbull, of the
Department (Scotch) replied—* That is a question I
cannot answer.”’

Very Rev. Dean Carmody said some plain things in
a plain” manner at another meeting of the Technical
Committee. Iere are a few of them :—* He objected
to be ridden over rough-shod by the Department.”
“They were treated as if they were foreigners and
strangers, and were not concerned at all in the interests
of the town.” T must say it, and I have no hesita-
tion in saying it, that the Department singled him out
because he was a Catholic.”

Now the question as it appears to us is—Is an effi-
cient teacher to be shifted because he is an “ Idolator,””
and because his position is wanted by an imported
Scotchman? There is a deadlock now, and the schools
are not going on. We have no doubt that the Tralee
Technical Committee will stand to its guns, and not
allow itself to be intimidated by the Dublin Castle
New Department over which Mr. Walter Long, the
Orangemen’s Chief Secretary, presides.

We have received the following letter : —‘‘ Limerick,.
3/10/°05. Sir—The Irish Protestant, instead of pub-
lishing my reply to its first attack, publishes a further
attack, signed ‘ Vigilance,” perhaps you can find room
for my reply. No wonder the writer of that absurd
letter does not care to sign his name, as it is too patent
an attempt to foster the spirit of disunion and bigotry
in Ireland.—Yours truly, R. Gisson.”

We are not surprised that the organ edited by the
Grand Master of ‘‘ Independent’ Orangemen refused
to print Mr. Gibson’s letter. The reply of Mr. Gibson
to the second attack in our funny but far from pros-
perous contemporary has already appeared in a daily
paper, but we will not be surprised to hear that it,
like the previous letter, is denied the columns of the
paper for which it was specially written.

Mr. P. J. Fleming, 5 Emmet Street, Killarney,
Hon. Sec. of Feip na Muran requests us to inform
the Prize Winners at the recent Munster Feis and
Industrial Exhibition that as the judges’ returns do
not supply addresses that he would be obliged if they
would communicate with him as soon as possible, men-
tioning the competitions in which they won prizes.

The Imperial Combine are not slackening their efforts
to crush out the Irish tobacco manufacturers. Should
their plans succeed the Irish retail tobacconists, not to
speak for the moment of the other important interest
affected in this country, are likely to be in a bad way.
Monopolies when firmly established are not usually
philanthropic in their working, and should the gallant
fight now being made by the independent firms be
overborne higher prices and shorter credit will pro-
bably be the fate in store for the retail trade. We
understand that the most recent device employed by
the combine to got the Irish retailers under their
thumb is that of sending an ‘‘ expert’’ to dress their
windows. This means, we learn, that the windows of
such firms as submit to the humiliating treatment are
almost exclusively decked out with the various brands.
of tobacco, cigarettes, ctc., in which the combine are
interested. Bub as we are a very green people a dab
or two of green paint may be pubt on here and there
in the shape of a few Irish-made articles which are
however practically hid amid the profusion of the
foreign display. The combine’s ““expert’’ is, we are

informed, now on tour, and the windows of many

In the course of the annual report presented by the 
Hon. Sec., Mr. Maurice O'Brien, at the annual general 
meeting of the Carrick-on-Suir branch of the Gaelic 
League, he says:-" This change of feeling in Carrick 
at any rate is, in my opinion, to be attributed to the 
splendid manner in which the teaching of the Irish 
language has been taken up in the schools of the town. 
Irish is being taught. in every school in the town-
male and female. 
Not alone are the pupils learning 
Irish, but they are taught their country's history and 
to respect the traditions of their forefathers. , The 
children take to the study of the language most en-
thusiastically, a fact which is in no small way clue_ lo 
the splendid spirit which animates the heads of the 
schools." 
The Cork Industrial Development Society are anxious 
to include in the second edition of their Directory of 
Irish Manufactures everything of interest to house-
keepers. 
A fair response has been received to their 
circular letter, but yet they have to state that "we 
know hundreds of firms, etc., throughout Ireland 
making goods which housekeepers require, who have 
not yet supplied us with the desired information." 
Those wbo have not replied to the Association's letter 
should do so at once, and address their communications 
to Mr. E. J. Riordan, Secretary, Cork Industrial .Asso-
. ciatiop, 13 Marlboro' Street, Cork. 
"Imaal" wrote an article on Mr. Colm's play, 
"The Land " when it was acted for the first time some 
months ago,' and we n~ed not n~w say anything ?f the 
performance of it which we witnessed, and enJoyed,

The literary and make-believe literary folk who haug 
round the Abbey Theatre believe, or make-believe, in 
sincerity. That is all the more r~ason that _we feel no

We confess that on the few occas10ns that we

Many of

It is not fair to the plays that some of the audience

oo THE SITUATION OF THE POLITICIANS.

R. JOHN REDMOND’S speech at Doon was an
N appeal for the political support of the country
as against the counter-claim of Mr. William O’Brien.
We watch the game with interest and—in the off-hand
sense in which the word is frequently used—with im-
partiality. ~ The political situation does not at all
satisfy us, but we have to take it, to a large extent, as
we find it, and so we are only concerned now with the
situation of the politicians. What we may call—and
our readers who have done the detail work and hand
to hand fighting will appreciate what we mean—what
we may call the LEADER movement has materially
affected the position of mere politics in the Irish
cconomy. Our readers had to fight the battle of the
Language and the battle of the Industrial movement in
the teeth of the orthodox politicians of three, four and
five years ago ; our readers are still left alone practically
to fight, against all kinds of opposition, in the cause
of Justice, Tolerance, and Fair Play for much of the
secular rights of Irish Catholics in their own country ;
and then Bung ‘‘stands in’’ with the politicians and
is a cheap patriot, and the politicians are tender of Mr.
Bung’s feelings.  Concerning a great many of the
national shortcomings of orthodox politicians we have
to be content with pursuing a kind of Fabian policy—
for the present.

In our view the party against Mr. O’Brien are a
rather indifferent lot standing on a poor platform; in
our view also Mr. William O’Brien, his paper, and
Augustine Roche are a very unsatisfactory collection,
and so we do not waste our time now writing on the
Political Situation ; we confine ourselves to the situation
of Politicians. Taking them all in all, bundling Mr.
John Dillon, who never made a protest: against bigotry
ab a meoting of tho Great Northern Railway, throwing
in the dead weight of the tame Freeman, the one man’s
]m’ponny-is-as-good-as—ano(;hor-man’s-]_\a’ponny Inde-
pendent, Mr. William O’Brien, with his Zrish People
and its advertisements of Bung and foreign boots, etc.
—taking them all in all they comprise in our view a
highly unsatisfactory collection not capable of facing
with vigour, thoroughness nor first-clags ability, the
living issues of the day. Bubt viewed that way as @
whole we must take them largely as we find them, for
it will take a long time to alter them. The immediate

question at present is—is it better for the countr
: tha_.t all thnt' slgould pull together in a rough workinz
unity, or split into two fragments to engage in mutual
abuse? Upon that question we have no doubt what-
ever.  Imperfect though they are, it would be a
calamity if they fell to abusing one another.

‘What does Mr. O’Brien want? We do not know.
We do not believe he knows himself, beyond that he
is'swayed by a crude desire that the Triumvirate and
others should be made to bite the dust, and that No. 7
Lower Abbey Street should become the hub of the Irish
race at home and abroad. We desire neithor consum-
mation.

. One essential quality for an offective man of affairs
is that he should have a broad working understanding
of human nature. Mr. O’Brien’s understanding is
thickly clouded by his strong emotions, and he sces
Sexton, Davitt and Dillon now walking this planct in
. cloven hoofs, and with tails imperfectly hidden from
view. We do not see a tail wagging from John Dillon
any more than we see wings on William O’Brien; we
consider that two clever strong men of LEaDER prin-
ciples would weigh both of them down. Mr. O’Brien
flaunts Bung in the advertising pages of his paper, and
in face of the struggle of the Irish boot manufacturers,
of firms like Winstanley, Governey, of Carlow, Hearne
and Cahill, of Waterford, and the Lee Firm of Cork,
he plants, for so much an inch, an announcement in
the left-hand top corner of his paper that a certain
make of British boots ‘‘are the best.”” We do not
see that anything is to be said for killing the Trium-
virate in order to make William O’Brien Qdpo 13,
but there is a lot to be said why, faulty as the whole
combination is, the country should not be distracted
by a campaign of abuse and recrimination between
tweedilites and twaddleites.

Up to this, as far as we can see, those attacked by
Mr. O’Brien have displayed considerable self-restraint,
.and talk of the ‘‘ vile man hunt’’ type has been con-
fined to Mr. O’Brien’s side; and this notwithstanding
Mr. O’Brien’s own historic dictum that Parnell could
xot be fought with sugar-sticks.

The Zrish Times is rubbing its libellous hands with
glee at the prospect of a split. If we had a daily
paper we would not be long before we froze the anti-
Irish heart of Alf Fox with fear and trembling. The
tame Freeman that we have is a poor thing, and the
Independent has only a ha'penny heart. A daily
Leaper would clarify the situation and supply a
driving power that would stimulate into activity every-
thing healthy and progressive in the country. But
where’s the use of talking. We have not a daily
LEADER, and for the present we must take things as
‘they are and face the facts of the situation, including
the Simply Deplorable.

We read Mr. Redmond’s appeal at Doon for the
“support of the politicians with sympathy. We hope in
due time that the politicians as a body will be altered
for the better, but it would suit no people’s game hetter
than those for whom the anti-Irish Z7ish Times speaks

and alter the politicians of Ircland to preserve them
as a working entity, subject to a free _ﬂow of criticism,

MODERN LIGHT ON THE ANCIENTS.

WENT to see the “ Gallery of Modern Art,”” ab pre-

sent housed in the Kildare St. Muse'um,_ and I
purchased a catalogue of the pictures which it com-
‘prises. Mr. Hugh P. Lanc has written what he calls
“an “ Explanation ”’ as a sort of inhrodugbml} to the cata-
“logue, and a very remarkable oxplanation it is. J:Iere
is a passage, in which the italics. are mine :— For
many years we have had at the National Gallery a very
-fine collection of ancient pictures; yet to understand
ancient pictures, it is mecessary o have first scen a great

* many modern pictures.” Why! I confess this dictum
-staggers me. Does Mr. Lane mean by"“ancient pic-
tures ”’ such. works as those of Raffaelle or Murillo?.

THE

LEADER.

And by “modern pictures’’ does he mean those of, say,
Monet and Degas? I utterly deny that the works of
theso gentlemen help me to understand Titian or Raf-
faclle the least bit better than before, though I confess
that the contrast makes me like the “ ancients’’ a great
deal betiter than before! Can I not understand a Velas-
quez until T have seen a Puvis de Chevannes? If that
be so, how did the Spaniard gain his great reputation
conturies before the “moderns’’ were born? Presum-
ably by a parity of reasoning the works of Monet will
only be understood hundreds of years hence, when he is
himself an “ancient,”” by the light of the works of the
fubure “ moderns,”” whose own works, in turn, will have
to be illuminated by the ‘“ moderns "’ of some still far-
ther future! But there is more.

“The National Gallery spoke a language not to be
understood by ordinary people; and in the world of
pictures everything stood still for want of a modern
gallery.”  Well, for my part, I can understand the
language of the National Gallery well enough; but
then, perhaps, I am not “ordinary people.”’ As for
saying that “in the world of pictures everything stood
still for want of a modern gallery ”’—that will not argue
out. In this very collection we have an excellent bit
by the late Walter Osborne, done before this Modern
Gallery was ever talked about, and there is a piece of
his of a similar genre in the Tate Gallery, London;
how are these things done without the inspiration of a
Modern Gallery? How were the pieces of Yeats and
Tone done? And the seascape of Edwin Hayes in
the Tate Gallery? I am not arguing against having a
Modern Gallery, mind; all I ask is, don’t plead on a
wrong basis for it. Art represents Nature, which is, at
all times, the same, and never “ancient’’ nor
“modern.’”” If the works of latter day artists can
stand on their own merits as presentations of Nature,
let us have them; if they can’t let us not have them.
Consider what nonsense it would be to suggest that in
order to understand Shakespeare rightly, we should first
read a lot of Ibsen and Pinero! Pictures are like
plays; they are valuable in themselves, or not at all.
The dramatist who is only valuable as an illustrator of
some old dramatist is of no value at all. “People,”
says Mr. Lane, “would go to see modern pictures, and
afterwards with some degree of intelligence and enjoy-
ment go to see masterpieces painted some hundreds of
years ago.”” Perhaps so; but woe betide the man who
comes to see some of these Modern Gallery pictures ‘n
the hope of picking up “ some degree of intelligence *’—
say No. 74, “ The Banquet,”’ by Monticelli! The man
who can only like the Rembrandts and the Van Dycks
in the National Gallery after a study of Monticelli must
be a very mad man, indeed. But it is time to say a few
words about the pictures themselves.

To criticise all the pictures justly is impossible while
they are in their present locus. Many of the works re-
quire to be seen at a due distance; in their present sta-
tion this is impossible. Another thing is that the re-
flections from the glass-cases—necessary features of a
museum, of course—render a proper view quite impos-
sible. Still something may be said. Most of the Corots
and Constables are all right; not all,-though.  Some
of the former are mnot characteristic of the painter;
others are trivial or mediocre. M ust we worship every-
thing with “ Corot ’ on it? I do not fecl able to. No-
body pretends that al/l Shakespeare’s plays—Titus
Andronicus, for instance—arc master-pieces; why pre-
tend that everything with “ Corot ™’ on it is priceless?
Some of the things of his in this collection could be done
without, and not grievously missed, either. The same"
applies to some of the Constables, not z.mll of which are
oither characteristic or superb. The pieces of Whist-
ler’s would nover be missed; he would never have
made his name by these. I sce no good in Daumicr’s
bit (No. 2). As for Rousseau’s Moonlight,”” it is cle-
verly done, but who wants to see such things done at
all?” I don’t? All Fantin-Latowr’s things are all
right, but are not most of them trifling? Mancini’s
things would look botter at a distance; “En Voyage’’
sooms the best. Most of Wilson Steer’s pieces are all
right. £550 are asked for Courbet’s “ Dans le Foret ;'

BELFAST AND BIGOTRY,—IV.

“The spirit of volunteering was nowhere more con-
spicuous than in the Ulster Plantation; armed liberty
walked most boldly amongst the scenes of former con-
fiscation.”

—MAacNEvVIN (“ Voluntcers.”’ )

T O the Anglo-Trish mind the history of the Irish

Era of Independence is somewhat perplexing
reading. The Glorious Revolution is over, and “the
Irish enemie’’ has Jbeen under the feet of the Loyal
Minority of Planters—Cromwellian and other—for the
best part of a century. Suddenly the Planters,
alarmed by rumours of threatened invasion by the
French or other enemy of Great Britain, spring to arms
as Volunteers to save Ireland for England—even as
their grandfathers had done ninety years before. Then,
at the turn of a hand, as though the feel of arms in
their hands electrified them, they wheel their cannons
round, and direct them against the same England with
printed threats of “ Free Trade or else——!"’ ete., de-
pendent from the muzzles. Next they meet in conven-
tion at Dungannon, and unanimously resolve that they
are Irishmen. And then, after England has granted
their demands of Free Trade and a free Parliament,
they, as Volunteers, dissolve out of view as mysteri-
ously as they had appeared, and, as the Loyal Mino-
rity, quietly resume duty at their old trade. Nothing
very Irish about all this, one would say!

No Irishman, I take it, wants ta quarrel with the
favourable things all writers of Irish sympathies have
written of the -Volunteers. ~ For Planters, they acted
with - uncommon patriotism and broad-mindedness—
much more patriotically and broad-mindedly than
could well have been expected, bearing in mind their
history. That they did not, however, comply with
their own resolution, passed at a heated moment, and
become Irish, is evidenced by the fact that, after a
few years, they dropped back into the old groove, and
remained in it ever since. Their rising out was a mag.
nificent pout, and ended as pouts usually end. Thex
had not become Irish; it was only England’s step-
motherly treatment of them, sketched in the last chap-
ter, that vexed them into proclaiming themselves, and
in fact into fancying themselves, Irishmen—Irishman
being at all times the equivalent of enemy of England.
The example of their exiled brethren—who had learned
to think clearly since they had crossed to America—at
the same time suggested to them how to go about mak-
ing England listen to what they had to say. The pity
is that England had, just at tho moment, to listen, and
could not;.ﬁgh(;. Had the Volunteers been forced to
fight as Irishmen they would, whether victorious or de-
foated, very likely have become Irish, politically on the
spot, and in due courso nationally and in full rveality.

My concern with the Volunteers in these papers is
that it was the Scotch Planters in Ulster that starbed
tho movement, and from fivst to last led and inspired it.
The arming started in Belfast. The Dungannon Con-

* vention was a Convontion of the Ulster Delegates, and

was composed, as an issue of the News Letter of the date
records,, “almost ontirely of Dissenters.”” This Con-

the people of Ireland invincible.”” Precisely—all but
the littlo word “will.”” Oh, that word “ will*” in Irish
history! By Davis’s time we had retired from Father
O’Donnell’s position back on “it were a glorious deed
to make Ireland invincible by a union of creeds and
races. In our own day, all that is left for us to say
is, that the making of Ireland invincible on said lines
has not been done. And as it has not been, and is not
being done, the Catholics are waking up to a sense of
their own responsibilities in the matter. It were about
timoe they did. They have lost much valuable time,
and all that timo means, by standing idle, leaning,
with mouths open, on their shovels, waiting for the
other side to come up and resume the 1782 business of
the bridging of the Boyne.

Seriously, the enthusiastic burst of patriotism on the
part of the Volunteers raised a cloud that has obscured
the Irish issue ever since. They were our first Protes-
tant patriots.  Up to their time Irishman and Catho-
lic were synonymous in Ireland. Notwithstanding the
action of the Volunteers, and of a few like them since,
Irishman and Catholic are synonymous in the Ireland
of to-day. The Catholics welcomed the awakening of
patriotism in the Volunteers; they have heartily wel-
comed any Protestantysince who offered to join the Irish
ranks—aye, or to lead'them if he had a talent that way.
They have done their part towards a union of creeds.
The fact remains that the creeds are not united. Whose
fault is it? We have over a century of proof whose
the fault is, and the Irishman—or Catholic, it is all the
same—would be a fool if he continued much longer to
shut his eyes to the fact that the Protestant minority
are not, and have no wish to become, Irish. O’Connell
was fond of saying that the Irish Protestant Parlia-
ment would, only for the Union, have emancipated the
Catholics long before 1829. Probably it would. And
possibly, but for the Union, the Irish émancipated Ca-
tholics, crowding into the Irish Parliament, would
have Irishised it in language and thought if not, which
would not matter, Catholicised it. The Union is a
fact, however, and whether or not it be the Union made
Unionists of, and kept English, the Protestant mino-
rity, that the Protestant minority is Unionist and Eng-
lish is a fact, too, and the Catholics have got to make
the best of it. To making the best of it a necessary
preliminary is to know exactly where they stand. To
help to map one corner of Ireland for them is the object
of the present writer. In another chapter he hopes to
have completed the outline. Then will come the filling
in with figures, etc. TaE O’NEILL.

CAHIR, OF “GALLANT” TIPPERARY.

IVE years ago the LEapEr had much up-hill work
before it, a good deal of hard plodding, more
perhaps than was anticipated, and many serious diffi-
culties to encounter in its endeavour to educate and
prepare the Nation to become an Irish Ireland. The
Sourface, the omnipresent “ Bung,” the “Rauthmines
Johnny,’’ the “Baunk Clerk,” were powers for evil in
the land, and stalked about with an air of ownership
and superiority and a contempt for the common crowd
that only readers of the LeAper thoroughly understand
and appreciate. These, on the one hand, had to be
cither entirely got rid of, or at least the contagion of
their evil influence had to be attended to and fought
against before any recal good could be done. On the
other hand, the people had to be educated and back-
bone put into them to make them understand and sneer
at the absurdity and hollowness of the creatures they
esteomed.

It is consoling, and Irish Irelanders are grateful, that
that work has in great measure been accomplished
wherever the Leaper is largely read and its spirit has
got a hold. Where it is not read, and where it has
got very little or no hold, almost the same state of
things prevails as before its coming, to the great dis-
honour and, as it were, contempt of Irish Ireland.
Such places surely ought to be exposed and pressure
brought to bear on them to make them fall into line, if

o les

THE LEADER.

We are to have Irish Ireland rule, as we hope, in every
town and village of the country.

Cahir, of “gallant *’ Tipperary, is one of such places.
It is a thoroughly West-British centre and a Bung
- stronghold—a fact that will explain why so few copies
of the Leaper make their way there weckly. I
do not know who reads them, but I presume it is some
of the priests, and the doctor, and a few of the other
principal men of the place. The common folk, God
help them in their present pitiable state, have little
relish for logic and strong and vigorous common-sense.
L'it-Bits, Pearsonw's Weekly, dAnswers, Novelettes, Home
Chat, The Family Reader, The Lady's Friend, and
Comic Cuts, ete., otc., suit their palate better, and they
are satisfied. As a rosult I am not surprised at what
appeared in a recent number of the Leaper—* ‘Gallant’
Tipperary again. The members of the Cahir Amateur
Dramatic Club recently played ‘Con O’Carrollan’s
Dream,’ and a laughable farce’ by the name of ‘Paddy’s
Mistakes.” One H. Connor, a ‘Saved’ ex-policeman,
was ‘Con O’Carrollan’; a Mr. Dickson, a ‘Saved’
school-master, was a general of the English Army; a
Mr. C. Condon, a ‘Bung,” was a magistrate. During
a concert that was perpetrated, a Mr. M. Lonergan
sang a parody on ‘Bill Bailey.” A Mr. P. O’Mahony
warbled ¢ Good-bye, Blue Bell.” We learn that
‘ Paddy’s Mistake ’ was produced by kind permission of
Abel Haywood and Sons, Manchester. There was once
upon a time a branch of the Gaelic League in Cahir,
in ‘Gallant’ Tipperary; where is it now? We hear
that they play < Hockey,” at Cahir, and when we con-
sider the class of theatrical fare they go in for we are
nos_surprised.’’

. Worse things, however, than are alluded to in that
paragraph, are not of rare occurrence in Cahir. I re-
member visiting the place one evening more than a year
ago, and certainly I was not edified on the occasion.
It happened that the Coons, or others of that ilk, were
travelling by the same train to give an “ entertainment’’
there. A short distance outside Clonmel, they began
2 most painfully boisterous shouting which they kept
up for fully five minutes. Having finished that, they
began to “sing,’”’ each a different melody. Some of
the words that reached my ears were shockingly im-
moral, and the “melodies’’ were, all that I could
distinguish of them, exceedingly suggestive. = Then
they started again to shout, and roar, and screech, and
so prepared themselves to “entertain’’ their Cahir
friends, a goodly number of whom met them at the
station to welcome and escort them to the Town Hall,
which I heard was over-crowded for the occasion and
.hundreds were refused admittance.

If there were a good working branch of the Gaelic
League in the place, “entertainments’’ of that kind,
which are not of rare occurrence, would certainly get
a blow. But they have no branch of the Gaelic League
in Cahir. They bhad something they called a branch,
which, however, did not survive. It was a novel thing
to start a branch, and so the young folk ventured, but
when the novelty began to wear away and work had
to be done, the members began to fall off one by one,
till eventually it was found impossible to continue with
the few that remained. The truth is that in their
present state the great number of the young men of
Cahir, so far as Irish Ireland is concerned, are uneflu-
cated, good-for-nothing Johnnies, with very little vita-
lity in them for anything except aping the ways of indi-
viduals of the Tommy Atkins’ type or his friend,
Johnny, from “Rauthmines.’’ y

Tt struck me as a curious thing, that on the occasion
of the public meeting of the United Irish League, held

_ in Cahir last December, when addresses were presented
from various places to Mr. John Redmond, from Clon-
‘mel, Tipperary, Cashel, Clogheen, Fethard, etc., etc.,
not ome was presented from Cahir. Several more or

influential persons made speeches, but I looked in
ain in the Freeman of the following morning for the
aport of a speech of any person from Cahir itself—all

he speakers were borrowed. God help us, when a
‘ahir man can’t even make a speech! . There were

‘bands from the neighbouring towns that played,

OcToBER 14, 1905.

I heard, some fine martial National airs. But Cahir
had no band of its own to show at the meeting. What
a want of vitality! Y

How truly disheartening and depressing when we con-
sider that the majority of the women are practically
worthless creatures also—readers of Novelettes, The
Lady’s Friend, T'he Family Reader, Comic Cuts, etc.,
atc. That many of them are utterly regard-
less of principle, this ought to show:—I re-
member a marriage of a Tommy Atkins Officer in
the Protestant Church not very long ago. Quite a
crowd of Catholic girls and young women went in to

‘see the coremony, and I heard that they were almost

beside the bridal party, paying the greatest attention,
of course, to the London and Paris fashions, as well as,
and there’s the rub, to the observance of the Protestant
Ritual, utterly regardless of the law of the Church for-
bidding them to be there.

Cahir, I said, is a “Bung’’ stronghold. ~ For the
2,058 inhabitants of this town of “ Gallant ’’ Tipperary,
there arve no less than about thirty-three or thirty-five
“bonded stores’’ that cost the town’s people and their
neighbours over £12,000 yearly, a fact that made me
often wonder does every man, woman and child in
Cahir drink “bung.”” I should not be surprised if
I were told they do. Sunday is a thirsty day in many
places in Ireland. It is especially so in Cahir. I
spent several Sundays there, and the amount of illicit
drinking I saw might waken up some of the officials
of Dublin Castle did they know of it, notwithstanding
the fact that several policemen in uniform paraded the
streets nearly all day, and.others in plain clothes “lay
in ambush ’’ patiently waiting for a catch. But the
drinking went on all the same. Men and boys, and
women and children passed into the beer shops and had
their “bung’’ at the counter or took it away in jugs,
or cans, or kettles, seemingly in peace. I remember
a woman coming out of a particular “bungery’’ about
three o’clock one Sunday, carrying a large black kettle
which was evidently too full. She walked on before me
now and then spilling a little of the contents, plainly
showing, poor woman, that she was not going to have
her tea. On she went in the broad day-light spilling
the bung on the public footpath, and yet she wasn’t
“ captured.” Now, the fellows in plain clothes must
be the simplest individuals in creation to allow that old
crone to come out of a beer shop on a Sunday afternoon
with a dirty black kettle in her hand and go her way
unchallenged. What would become of us as a Nation
if “Bung’’ ruled in every town in Ireland as it does
in Cahir  The police are sworn to do their duty and
so to enforce the observance of the Licensing Law. But
in Cahir, be assured the Licensing Law is -“more
honoured in the breach than the observance.’’

Before any good can be done, I believe the inhabi-
tants of Cahir must be entirely transformed by infusing
into them the spirit of the Leaper—the spirit of Irish
Ireland—which will give them vitality and back-bone
and sufficient common sense to enable them to see and
overcome the absurdity of their position. There are
at least half-a-dozen TIrish Irelanders in Cahir who
could, I think, without much difficulty arvange for
say half-a-dozen lectures on matters of intercst to Irish
Ircland, e.g., on the Industrial Movement, Irish His-
tory and Language, Past and Present Bigotry, Irish
Music, Irish Sports, etc., etc. These things would set the
people a-thinking, and instead of the Cahir Amateur
Dramatic Club giving “ Con O’Carrollan’s Dream *’ we
might some day in the future hear of an Irish play
being acted and Irish songs sung by Irish Irelanders in,
perhaps, a new Town Hall of Irish stone and workman-
ship. A spirit of independence and self-respect might
be fostered that would teach * Bung ’’ a lesson ho sadly
needs and make it impossible for the scandal of Sunday
drinking to continue. When these things come to pass
we shall have witnesses of Cahir’s awakening.

/ CLONMORE.

.S one intimately connected with the first Dublin

ments which "J.II.N." writes of.

cial gatherings about four or five times a year are a

ing and sympathy -amongst them which is so essential

t.o create this 
feeling .amongst the members of our

" Aind then he goes on to provide for the fm·ther in-
tention that these same tenants, educated in these 
schools, are to be prepared for admission to Dublin Uni- • 
versity.

tiou by himself at the first moment, and when founding
